policy_statement: Facilitate collective self-consumption projects in privately rented housing to combat energy poverty.
mechanism_description: The city is facilitating collective self-consumption projects in rented private housing so more on-site renewable electricity can be used locally, displacing fossil-based grid electricity and helping reduce energy poverty.
primary_causal_pathway: Distributed renewable self-consumption enabling → ↓ grid electricity demand (marginal fossil displacement) → ↓ power-sector GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Enables/expands collective self-consumption arrangements (shared PV/renewables across tenants/units/buildings) so more renewable electricity is generated and consumed locally rather than exported or unused.  
2) Mediating physical process: Local renewable generation substitutes for electricity that would otherwise be supplied by the grid; at the margin this reduces output from fossil-fueled generators (and associated upstream fuel-cycle emissions).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂e emissions from electricity generation (and potentially reduced peak demand), contributing to mitigation; social outcome of reduced energy bills/energy poverty is a co-benefit.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing grid-supplied (often fossil-marginal) electricity by increasing locally consumed renewable generation, thereby lowering GHG emissions from power generation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Facilitate shared on-site renewable use → reduced grid electricity consumption → reduced marginal fossil generation → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although it improves affordability (resource/justice co-benefit) and may slightly improve resilience, the main climate-relevant effect across such policies is emissions reduction from fossil electricity displacement, which is Mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the grid is already near-zero carbon, the mitigation benefit shrinks and the policy may look primarily like an equity/affordability measure; however the mechanism as defined is explicitly “fossil generation displacement,” keeping mitigation dominant in typical deployment. Rebound (increased consumption due to lower bills) could reduce net savings but usually does not reverse the primary intent/pathway.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that enables collective self-consumption with the goal/effect of displacing fossil-based grid electricity follows the same mitigation pathway regardless of housing tenure (rented/owned) or program design.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy is best tagged with specific subtypes (e.g., distributed solar, energy communities, energy poverty alleviation, tenant-landlord split incentives) depends on policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In very low-carbon grids, the dominant climate effect may shift toward resource affordability rather than emissions reduction; additionally, if exported renewable power is already fully credited/displaces fossil generation similarly, “collective self-consumption” may mainly affect distributional outcomes rather than total GHGs. Rebound and accounting/marginal emissions assumptions can also affect realized mitigation magnitude.
key_indicators: “facilitating the implementation”; “collective self-consumption projects”; “private housing it rents out”; “combat energy poverty”
co_benefits: Reduced energy bills / alleviation of energy poverty; potential social equity and health/wellbeing improvements from improved household energy affordability.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Facilitating” is ambiguous: it could include incentives (grants) or direct infrastructure investment (PV installation) in the municipal rental stock, but the excerpt does not specify funding or construction—so it is classified as an enabling planning/implementation support instrument rather than investment or incentive.
